symptoms.symptom_name: Progressive reduced consciousness culminating in unrousable coma

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Depressed consciousness correlates with severity of hyperosmolality; coma is characteristic of severe HHS.
Reduced consciousness can occur in large stroke, but is non-specific and better explained here by hyperosmolality.

symptoms.symptom_name: Two weeks of polyuria and polydipsia with severe fatigue

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The insidious osmotic-diuresis prodrome over days to weeks is typical of HHS and distinguishes it from the more rapid onset of DKA.

symptoms.symptom_name: Profound dehydration — sunken eyes, parched cracked lips, dry mouth

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Extreme dehydration from prolonged osmotic diuresis is central to HHS pathophysiology.

symptoms.symptom_name: Bedside capillary glucose above meter ceiling (>33.3 mmol/L); ketones 0.4

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Extreme hyperglycaemia with low/absent ketones is the biochemical hallmark of HHS versus DKA. Patient-obtained home-meter reading, unverified, but strongly corroborative.
Ketones of 0.4 are near-normal and argue against clinically significant ketoacidosis.
Hyperosmolality can produce transient focal neurological signs (stroke mimic) without vascular occlusion.
A focal deficit mandates emergency assessment to exclude stroke, even when a metabolic cause is more likely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Repeated missed oral hypoglycaemics over the prior week

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Non-adherence to glucose-lowering therapy is a recognised precipitant of HHS.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of vomiting, abdominal pain, and Kussmaul (deep laboured) breathing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Kussmaul breathing, vomiting and abdominal pain are features of DKA; their absence supports a hyperosmolar rather than ketoacidotic picture.
Objective hypovolaemic shock — sealed clinician measurement; the AI Doctor infers circulatory compromise from carer-reportable features (unrousable, profoundly dehydrated), not a measured BP.

symptoms.symptom_name: Serum osmolality, sodium, glucose, ketones and renal function (venous bloods)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Calculated/measured hyperosmolality with marked hyperglycaemia and minimal ketosis confirms HHS — a hospital laboratory investigation, never available via telehealth.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Profound dehydration + reduced consciousness/focal signs + extreme hyperglycaemia (>30) + absence of significant acidosis/ketosis together define the hyperosmolar, non-ketotic emergency.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Emergency hospital bloods — glucose, osmolality, sodium/potassium, renal function, venous blood gas, ketones
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms HHS and guides cautious fluid/insulin resuscitation; obtainable only after emergency transfer, not via telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: CT brain (in hospital, to exclude stroke given the focal sign)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Focal neurological deficit warrants imaging to exclude a primary vascular event, alongside treating the metabolic emergency.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Repeat home glucose finger-prick as a management or diagnostic step by the carer
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The bedside reading is already above the meter ceiling and the patient is comatose — further home testing must not delay calling 000.
